medications.indication_rationale: Short-course topical for perianal itch/discomfort; source advised twice daily for up to 7 days.
medications.dose_route_frequency: apply to the perianal area twice daily for up to 7 days (as per source note)

medications.indication_rationale: Softens stool and reduces straining — the key to healing and prevention; source advised psyllium 1 tablespoon daily with ample water.
medications.dose_route_frequency: 1 tablespoon daily with plenty of water (as per source note)

medications.drug_name: prolonged potent topical steroid to the anus
medications.indication_rationale: Prolonged perianal steroid causes skin atrophy — limit to short courses.

behavioural_change_actions.specific_action: Increase dietary fibre and fluids; use a footstool and limit toilet-sitting to ~3 minutes; avoid straining and phone-scrolling on the toilet.
behavioural_change_actions.rationale: Reduces straining and venous pooling that drive haemorrhoids.

behavioural_change_actions.specific_action: Use moist wipes/bidet rather than dry paper; gentle perianal hygiene.
behavioural_change_actions.rationale: Avoids trauma to the fragile skin over the lump.

patient_education_points.point: This looks like an external haemorrhoid (swollen vein) from straining — the bright-red blood on the paper comes from it. Softening the stool with fibre and avoiding straining, plus a short course of the cream, should settle the bleeding and itch in a few days.
Because any rectal bleeding should be properly checked, please arrange an in-person examination, and bowel-cancer screening if you're due — even though this looks benign.

Go to ED if the lump becomes hard, dark purple and excruciatingly painful, if bleeding becomes heavy and drips/pours into the bowl, or if you pass black tarry stools, feel faint, or develop a fever. See a doctor for any change in bowel habit, weight loss, or blood mixed through the stool.

follow_up_plan.what_to_reassess: Resolution of bleeding; in-person exam; arrange bowel-cancer screening/colonoscopy as appropriate.
follow_up_plan.trigger_for_earlier_review: Thrombosis/heavy bleeding — ED; alarm features — GP/colonoscopy.

must_include_items: Screen colorectal alarm features and arrange in-person exam + age-appropriate bowel-cancer screening
Fibre/fluid + anti-straining toileting behaviour (footstool, limit sitting)
acceptable_alternatives: Any short-course topical anorectal preparation; sitz baths; any bulk laxative
errors_of_omission: Not screening alarm features / not arranging exam or screening for PR bleeding
errors_of_commission: Attributing all bleeding to haemorrhoids and closing without a malignancy safety-net
Sending an uncomplicated haemorrhoid with a negative screen to ED by ambulance
scoring_rubric.passing_threshold_notes: Pass requires a colorectal alarm-feature screen with in-person exam / screening arranged, fibre + anti-straining measures, and thrombosis/heavy-bleeding + malignancy safety-nets.
